title: BatchEnsemble: An Alternative Approach to Efficient Ensemble and Lifelong Learning
authors: Yeming Wen, Dustin Tran, Jimmy Ba (University of Toronto / Vector Institute / Google Brain)
tokenization_method: BatchEnsemble proposes NO tokenizer. It is an efficient-ensembling weight-parameterization mechanism. For each layer it keeps ONE shared 'slow' weight matrix W in R^{m x n} and gives each ensemble member i a rank-one 'fast weight' F_i = r_i s_i^T (r_i in R^m, s_i in R^n). The member's effective weight is the Hadamard product W_i = W . (r_i s_i^T). The full forward pass over a whole layer for member i vectorizes to Y = phi( ((X . R) W) . S ): pre-multiply the input elementwise by R, apply the SHARED matmul W, then post-multiply elementwise by S. So all members share the single expensive matmul W and differ only by two cheap elementwise (broadcast) vector products. A mini-batch is split into M sub-batches (or repeated M times at test, effective batch B*M) so every member runs in one parallel batched pass. This is the antecedent of the per-feature-agnostic ensembling adapter that TabM later drops onto a tabular MLP.
feature_types_handled: Domain-agnostic at the layer level; the paper evaluates on images (CIFAR-10/100, ImageNet) and text/sequence (WMT translation), NOT tabular. It does not distinguish numerical/categorical/temporal/missing feature types itself — it operates on whatever dense activations the backbone produces.
token_granularity: N/A — BatchEnsemble does not define tokens. It parameterizes layer WEIGHTS, not inputs. (When transferred via TabM, the per-feature embeddings are the tokens and BatchEnsemble only multiplies the backbone weights.)
high_cardinality_handling: N/A — does not touch input encoding, so it neither helps nor hurts high-cardinality categoricals on its own. Any high-cardinality handling must come from whatever tokenizer/backbone it wraps.
temporal_handling: None as a tokenizer. Applied to Transformer self-attention layers for WMT (each ensemble member keeps its own attention via per-member fast weights), but this is sequence modeling, not time-varying-feature fusion.
scales_to_many_features: Indirectly relevant: cost is dominated by the single shared matmul, so an M-member ensemble adds only the rank-one vectors (m+n params per layer per member) plus two elementwise products. ResNet-32 BatchEnsemble of size 4 = ~10% more params vs 300% (3X) for a naive ensemble; Split-ImageNet over 100 tasks = 30M vs 25M params (20% overhead). Test-time cost ~1X a single model vs M X for naive. The mechanism's per-member overhead is O(m+n) (linear in layer width), independent of any input feature count — so it composes cleanly with a wide-feature backbone without blowing up.
downstream_model: Any layer-based neural net: ResNet-32 / WideResNet (vision), Transformer base/big (NMT), LeNet/ResNet-18/50 (lifelong). No tabular model in the paper; TabM (2024) is the tabular adaptation.
pretraining_objective: None. Trained end-to-end supervised on the target task (or sequentially per-task in the lifelong setting). The lifelong variant assigns one ensemble member (one r_t,s_t pair) per task while freezing the shared W after task 1, giving zero catastrophic forgetting with no memory buffer.
key_results: CIFAR (ResNet-32x4, ensemble size 4) validation accuracy: C10 95.94 (BatchE) vs 95.31 (single) vs 95.72 (MC-dropout) vs 96.30 (naive ensemble = upper bound); C100 80.32 vs 78.32 vs 78.89 vs 81.02. WMT14 big-Transformer perplexity on Newstest2013: EN-DE 4.26 (BatchE) vs 4.30 (single/MC-drop); EN-FR 2.74 vs 2.76/2.77; ~1.5X faster convergence. CIFAR-10-Corruptions: ECE between single and naive ensemble, far better than dropout ensemble. Lifelong: Split-CIFAR100 ~PNN accuracy with 4X speedup and 50X less memory; Split-ImageNet 100 tasks at 20% param overhead, beats BN-tuned baseline. Test-time speedup 3X and memory reduction 3X vs naive ensemble of size 4.
baselines_compared: Naive deep ensembles (independent networks; treated as accuracy upper bound), MC-dropout / dropout ensembles, single network, and for lifelong learning: Progressive Neural Networks (PNN), DEN, RCL, BN-Tuned.
transferable_idea: Rank-one multiplicative weight adapters (W . (r s^T)) shared atop one base matmul give you an M-member ensemble at ~O(width) extra params and ~1X compute. For the 70+-heterogeneous-feature setting this is the cheap regularizer/uncertainty wrapper to put AROUND any heterogeneous-feature tokenizer+backbone: it is tokenizer-agnostic, adds negligible cost, and (as TabM demonstrated) turns a plain wide-feature MLP into a top-tier tabular model. The diversity comes for free from random init of the r,s vectors.
limitations: (1) Splitting a fixed mini-batch across M members gives each member only 1/M of the data, so it needs ~50% more training iterations or an M X larger batch to match single-model data exposure. (2) Rank-one per-layer perturbation is strictly less expressive than independent members — naive ensemble remains the accuracy upper bound (C100 81.02 vs 80.32). (3) On WMT, lower validation loss did NOT translate to a higher BLEU score over the single model (metric-mismatch caveat). (4) Lifelong variant assumes task identity is known at test time (one member per task) and freezes the shared backbone after task 1, capping per-task capacity.
pitfalls: Not a tokenizer and not tabular — do NOT file it as a feature-encoding method; its only path into this topic is via the ensembling adapter that TabM (record: tabm-parameter-efficient-ensembling) ports to tabular MLPs. The headline '3X speedup/memory' is for ensemble size 4 specifically and is a test-time/storage claim, not a training-wall-clock claim (training is ~1.5X longer due to the data-split issue). The WMT BLEU non-improvement is an explicit example where the efficiency gain did not yield a downstream-metric gain — a relevant skeptic check before assuming the adapter always helps.
relevance_to_topic: Indirect but load-bearing: it is the originating mechanism behind TabM's parameter-efficient ensembling, the cheapest known way to wrap a wide-feature tabular tokenizer+backbone in an ensemble for accuracy/calibration with near-zero extra cost.
code_available: yes — original TF/Edward2 implementation by the authors (github.com/google/edward2, uncertainty_baselines); the tabular port lives in the TabM repo (github.com/yandex-research/tabm).
